## [00:00 - 03:02]

Welcome back to my lesson on limited liability companies. As I mentioned earlier, this is my jam. I love LLCs. Now I know different attorneys like different things and you were like, you're crazy. That's what you get excited for. And I'm like, I do. I get excited for LLCs. I don't know why. So what I found though is there's a lot of companies out there that will are law firms or CPAs or businesses that sell business formation. What they do is they set it up, but they don't really show you the inner workings of what an LLC is, how it's comprised, what makes up the foundation of an LLC. So today on this lesson, we're going to go through an LLC and I'm going to explain it in basic layman's terms. The better you understand the concepts of how LLCs and companies work and taxation and everything else we're talking about in this course and in this particular lesson, the better you're going to be able to run the foundation of your own business. You're going to take those concepts and ideas and you're going to put yourself on a better level to succeed because you understand the inner workings of businesses. Let's talk about what an LLC looks like. So first off, if I go out and I form an LLC, what does that mean? Well, I go to what's generally the state where you reside. Sometimes it's the secretary of state, sometimes the department of commerce depends on the state where you're at. I go there and I file an LLC with the state and it's generally under a document called articles of organization. That's the document that sets up the business and most of these are state specific and they're already outlined what you have to have in there. So kind of the language that the law in the state where we're going to set it up for is pretty well set, but there are a couple of items that we have to insert into there. The first item that we have to know is we need to know who the owners of this new LLC, who are they going to be? Is it going to be you individually or is it going to be multiple individuals and what do we even call them anyway? So unlike corporations that have shareholders, LLCs have what are called members. Members are the owners, right? So most of you, if you're just doing this by yourself, you're taking your course and you're going to run with it and you're going to advertise with it and you're going to take that knowledge and share it with others just by yourself, you're going to be what's called a single member LLC. Okay. Just by yourself. Now what are some of the other roles that you have? Well, in addition to members, we have to have people that are going to make the day-to-day decisions that are going to go out there and actually implement what you're trying to do. We call them managers. Oh, so I've got members and managers. That's my M&M for you. Okay. Members and managers. Managers are the day-to-day, you know, just like you think about having a service station or having a concierge service, you have a manager that oversees it, you're the general manager of the business. Okay. You're the manager. You get to go in and do things like open a bank account, make transactions for the business. You get authority to act on behalf of the business. So an LLC has members, managers.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

It also has another entity or individual called a registered agent. Now what's a registered agent? I get this all the time because people will say, well, hey, Greg, I want to set up a business, but this registered agent thing or this manager thing, if I act in that role and capacity, am I going to create additional liability for myself as an individual? And the answer is no. A registered agent is somebody who will accept legal notice on behalf of the organization. And some states like California, for example, if you don't want to designate somebody specifically, you can designate the secretary of state. And the secretary of state will receive legal notice for the business and then forward it to you as the manager or the member of the business itself. So on other states, you can actually hire a company. We use a company all the time. We have a service for individuals and business owners that want to set up LLCs. We have a service we can use for them to get a registered agent in all 50 states because we are a licensed law firm. We're licensed and we do a national practice. So we have licenses in 25 states and across the United States. We have a network in all 50 states and wherever you're at. For those of you that are out of the country, we can act as a registered agent for you. We set up a lot of businesses for individuals that are in Europe or in Africa, South America or North, in Canada. So wherever you're at, we can do that. And registered agents are just people to receive notification for the business itself, for the LLC itself. So, so far we've talked about three roles. So in order to file an articles of organization to start our LLC, we need to have members, managers and registered agents. And those are separate roles. They can sometimes be played by the same people. In fact, one of the first businesses I ever set up, I was the member, I was the manager and I was the registered agent. So you can perform all three functions if it's just you. There's nothing wrong with that. In fact, it's quite common. We do it quite often for our clients. But you don't have to. And we have a lot of different options. But understanding what the benefits of having those options. For example, say you have that knowledge course. Now I use this as an illustration in one of my prior lessons. Say you're out there doing a fitness health course and you're showing people how if they exercise and they watch their weight and they do things, that it can decrease some of the negative side effects of being obese and everything else like having lower cholesterol and maybe having a better heart rate and better overall blood sugar levels. And it's just different side effects of being in a better healthy lifestyle. If you're out there and you're doing that and you're selling that course and maybe you make a claim in your course that you create an impression that if people do that, that they can stop taking insulin for their diabetes. Now I would never advise you doing that, but say you did. Maybe you made a mistake and you said that and somebody saw that and they decided, oh, I'm going to stop taking my insulin and they die of kidney failure. And then their spouse, like I use in the other illustration, sues you for that.

## [06:03 - 09:03]

Well, if you're the registered agent, what will happen is somebody's going to, they're going to draft up a lawsuit and then what happens is you have to serve that lawsuit on either the company or the individual. What will happen even if you have an LLC and you're the registered agent is they could come and knock on your door at whatever time at night and then they would give you that document at your house. That's a registered agent. Now, not everybody wants to have that. So we have a service that we do for clients that we can have an outside third party act as your registered agent, but outside of being sued, you don't have any liability as a registered agent, but it's not very convenient sometimes to have somebody knock on your door at night and deliver those documents to you. So setting up an LLC, not complicated, not hard members, managers, registered agents, just understanding the role is important about the different ramifications. Now, how to use an LLC and what is that? So LLCs, as I mentioned earlier, are governed by every state's acts or statute. So every state has an LLC act and what you have is basic statutory provisions that are kind of general and broad. Now, maybe I don't like those statutory provisions and maybe I decide I want to enter into an LLC not just as a sole member LLC, but maybe I want to have a partner, a partner that's not my wife or spouse or whoever it is. Somebody else I want to do this business with. Well, I want to make sure that maybe I set the tone of that agreement so that it's not just default statutes that the state has decided, but I enter into a covenant and contract with that partner in what we call an operating agreement or in some cases an LLC agreement. Now what that does for me is it's basically a contract between me and the company. So if I'm doing it by myself and I'm just the only member, I contract with myself about how to operate that business. If I have a partner, I make sure that operating agreement handles all the disputes we might potentially have as a partnership. If it's two of you, how do you handle resolve ties when you're voting on things? What if your partner goes off the rails and does something you don't agree with and you need to get that partner out of the business? It talks about all of that versus just leaving the state statutes that generally don't really apply those principles really well. Now if I'm just out there by myself and I'm the only member, why would I need an operating agreement? Well, one of the first steps I'm going to talk about in my next lesson is when you set up an LLC is to go out and get a bank account. But if I go to the bank and I just say, hey, this is me and I don't show them the authority I have to open that account as the manager of the business, then they say, we're not going to do this for you. So I may need it to show authorization to a third party that I'm authorized on behalf of this LLC to do X, Y, or Z. So an operating agreement, even as a sole member, is super important. For all the other reasons, if you have multiple members, you want to make sure that you're handling disputes really well. And that's really where I see the biggest issue with LLC is if you don't have a good

## [09:03 - 10:46]

operating agreement, it doesn't talk about how to handle disputes. And if you don't have that in that operating agreement, you're kind of opening yourself to failure because it's going to open up to lawsuits and a whole bunch of litigation. And I can tell you this as an attorney, and I litigate cases, I defend and bring them that litigation and being in a lawsuit is not very efficient and is very expensive. Now an LLC also does something for us as well. Remember how I said it keeps it separate and distinct? Well a separate and distinct LLC, one of the ways we do that is we go to the IRS, we knock on their door, and we get a separate number for tax purposes. We call that an employer identification number or EIN. So we get that EIN, and when we open bank accounts or we file taxes, we don't file it under our social security number or for you that are out of the country, we have what's called an ITIN, which is an international tax identification number we can get for you in the United States. Instead of using that individual one, we get this EIN for the business. And that business does all the activities, opens bank accounts, does everything else under that number. The other thing it does is when we do this LLC, we don't have to worry about those corporate formalities I mentioned about corporations. We don't have to have annual showholder meetings. We don't have to send out notice. We don't have to keep minute minutes. We get to keep that and protect our business assets and our personal assets, keeping them separate. So, now I've gone through and I've briefly again brushed across the surface of what limited liability companies are. Obviously, there's much more to it. But I want to make sure in our next lesson to talk about not just what else these are, but how do you use them? So make sure you watch that because it's not just good enough to understand the inner workings of an LLC if you don't understand how to use an LLC.
